original_abstract: While some scholars suggest that decolonial and decentring approaches are complementary—with the former opening political spaces for the latter to implement pragmatic, systemic evolutions—this synergy is far from guaranteed. Such alignment is particularly unlikely if the two agendas remain conceptually conflated. Although decentring may enhance the legitimacy and efficacy of EU foreign policy, it risks maintaining, or even reinforcing, the underlying structures of colonial power. Consequently, research and policy must shift beyond the mere objective of "decentring Europe" toward decolonial futures that explicitly prioritize the democratic dismantling of colonial continuities.
humanized_abstract: Despite the potential harmony suggested between decolonial and decentring ideas—openings for the latter to put into practice concrete evolutionary processes in the international organisations and institutions managed by the former—the fact is that they are not harmonious as concepts are not clarified. While decentring may add to the EU’s capacity to legitimise and accomplish its foreign policy in a more plural manner, the colonial matrix could well continue or even increase, entrenching its dynamics. Therefore, instead of simply aiming for ‘decentring Europe’ —by the time when we already know all its effects and consequences — it is indispensable to work on thinking a decolonial future that enables a full-fledged democratic release of colonial ongoing relationships.
